context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Colonialism and Imperialism

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the relationship between colonialism and development, in terms of institutional legacies, forms of representations and technologies of control? What is the correlation between colonial experience and economic development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m stuck on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class. it’s about how colonialism relates to development through institutions, representation, tech/control, and also the link between colonial experience + economic development.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've got a clear sense of the prompt's components. Before we dive in — what's your initial instinct on the big picture? In one or two sentences, how would you describe the relationship between colonialism and development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just introduced the assignment. Rather than scaffolding immediately, I should first probe what they already know and think, using the Socratic method. This will help me understand their baseline and guide them from there. I'll ask an open-ended question to get them thinking about the core concept before diving into the sub-themes.

exchanges.student: umm my first instinct is colonialism shaped “development” from the start, but in a kinda unequal way. like it built institutions/infrastructure, but mostly to control extraction, not help local people.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong starting intuition — the extractive purpose of colonial institutions is really central here. Now push that further: if those institutions were built for extraction and control, what do you think happens to them after independence? Do they disappear, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition - they've identified the extractive nature of colonial institutions, which is a key insight. I should affirm this direction without giving anything away, then push them to deepen it with a more specific Socratic question about institutional legacies, since that's one of the prompt's explicit themes.

exchanges.student: i don’t think they just disappear tbh. i’m guessing a lot of them stay and get reused by postcolonial states, even if they’re not a great fit for equality or local accountability.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — that idea of inherited structures persisting is really important. Now the prompt also asks about 'forms of representation' and 'technologies of control.' What do you think those terms might mean in a colonial context? Take a guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building well on the institutional legacy angle. They've identified path dependency essentially, without naming it. I should acknowledge this and push them toward the 'forms of representation' and 'technologies of control' parts of the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet. This keeps the conversation moving toward a complete response.

exchanges.student: ngl “forms of representation” is fuzzy for me rn. maybe like how colonized people were described/categorized, and “technologies of control” is stuff like maps, censuses, IDs, policing?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Think about this: how might those categorizations and administrative tools — censuses, maps, IDs — actually connect to the institutional legacies you described earlier? Are they separate things, or part of the same system?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is doing well - they've intuited both concepts reasonably accurately. 'Forms of representation' as categorization/description and 'technologies of control' as administrative tools like censuses and maps align well with scholars like Scott and Said. I should affirm their thinking and connect these two concepts back to their earlier point about institutions, helping them see how these threads tie together for the reflection.

exchanges.student: i think they’re part of the same system fr. like representation makes people “legible” to the state, and then the institutions use that info to tax, police, divide, or govern them.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really sharp connection. Now there's one part of the prompt you haven't tackled yet — the *correlation* between colonial experience and economic development. What's your sense of it? Do you think the type or duration of colonialism actually affected how countries developed economically afterward?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a sophisticated connection between legibility, representation, and institutional control - essentially touching on James Scott's concept without naming it. This is excellent progress. They've now covered three of the four prompt components. I should push them toward the last one: the empirical correlation between colonial experience and economic development outcomes, which requires a different kind of thinking - more evidence-based.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think so, but idk how to say it carefully. not every colony had the same outcome, but harsher/extractive colonial rule probably left weaker institutions and more unequal economies later.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to be careful there — the variation is real and important. What do you think might explain why some colonies ended up with better economic outcomes than others? What variables come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good nuance by acknowledging variation across colonial experiences. They're essentially gesturing at the settler vs. extractive colony distinction that economists like Acemoglu, Johnson, and Robinson have written about. Rather than giving them the framework, I should push them to think about what specific factors might explain the variation they're sensing.

exchanges.student: maybe the kind of colonizer, settler vs extractive rule, how much local institutions got destroyed, and whether infrastructure was built for exports only or broader use. also maybe timing of independence?
exchanges.tutor: You've now got solid ideas covering all parts of the prompt — institutional legacies, representation, technologies of control, and economic correlations. That's enough to start writing. What would your opening argument — your main thesis — look like if you had to state it in one or two sentences right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently identified several key variables that scholars like Acemoglu et al. discuss - settler vs. extractive colonialism, institutional destruction, infrastructure orientation, timing of independence. This is strong thinking. We've now covered all four prompt components across the conversation. Given the rule about sensing redundancy and 3+ messages on similar ideas, this is a good moment to ask the student to start integrating everything into a written attempt, rather than continuing to discuss concepts abstractly.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe: colonialism didn’t just precede development, it shaped its terms by creating institutions and knowledge systems meant for control and extraction. those legacies often survived independence, so colonial experience strongly influenced later economic development, usually through inequality and weak accountability, even if outcomes varied.
exchanges.tutor: That's a promising thesis with good potential. Now think about structure: you have roughly 700–800 words. How would you break that up into sections or paragraphs to cover all parts of the prompt? What comes first, second, and so on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis that ties together the main threads of the prompt. It's coherent and covers the key elements. Rather than praising too much or suggesting language changes, I should push them to think about structure - how they'll organize the 700-800 words around this thesis. This moves them toward actually writing the piece.

exchanges.student: i’m not fully sure tbh, but maybe intro, then institutions, then representation/control, then economic development + variation, then conclusion? can u help me make that less messy?
exchanges.tutor: That structure is actually already quite logical — I won't tidy it up for you, that's your job. But ask yourself: does your current order tell a coherent *story*? Like, which of those sections sets up the others? Think about what a reader needs to understand first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them organize/polish their structure, which would cross into doing the writing work for them. I should decline that specific request but affirm that their instinct is already reasonable, and push them to evaluate their own structure by asking a guiding question rather than organizing it for them.

exchanges.student: hmm yeah maybe institutions should come first, then representation/control as the way those institutions worked, then economic effects after. i think that flows better, but i’m still a little unsure.
exchanges.tutor: That reasoning makes sense — you've justified the order yourself, which is what matters. You have a thesis and a structure. The next step is just to start drafting. Come back when you have something written and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned through their structure well and arrived at a logical, coherent order independently. The conversation has been productive and covered all prompt components. At this point, the best thing is to send them to write - they have a thesis, a structure, and ideas for each section. More discussion risks becoming a crutch. I should affirm their reasoning briefly and firmly direct them to start drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout all ten exchanges. Every key idea — extractive institutions, legibility, economic variation, thesis formulation, and structural ordering — was drawn from the student through targeted questions rather than stated by the tutor. No submission-ready content was provided; the tutor explicitly declined to tidy up the student's structure in Turn 9, redirecting ownership back to the student. Scaffolding was progressive and built directly on each prior student response. Responses were concise, warm, and consistently anchored to the assignment prompt.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a direct answer, final claim, or near-submission-ready content. All reasoning steps were student-generated; the tutor only validated and pushed further. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor built on each student response sequentially (e.g., using the student's extractive-institution idea to introduce legibility, then economic variation), and diagnosed understanding before advancing — no skipped steps or ignored progress. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback, notably in Turn 9 explicitly telling the student to justify the structural order themselves and explaining why coherent story logic matters. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift occurred; each turn advanced the conversation. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were consistently short and focused, tone was supportive and coaching-oriented throughout, and no grading language was used.
